summary: O'Neil proposes that smartphone apps and video games have fundamentally altered social behavior by keeping people, especially young men, indoors and disengaged from both street life and civic participation. She suggests this 'app effect' may partially explain declining crime rates alongside other factors like abortion access and reduced lead exposure. The post argues that digital entertainment functions as a 'pacifier for grown-ups' that keeps populations docile and distracted. While acknowledging some benefits, O'Neil worries that we've trained people to retreat into pointless activities that serve mainly to distract and collect marketing data.
key_points: Crime rates may have fallen partly because potential troublemakers are now gaming indoors rather than congregating on hot summer streets
Political engagement has declined as people choose Candy Crush over newspapers and civic participation
Digital devices pacify people in situations that would previously spark social interaction or collective action
The distraction comes with a hidden cost: data collection for marketing purposes
The thought experiment of apps disappearing reveals our dependency on digital pacification
money_quotes: I have a theory which I'm slightly embarrassed about but whatever, that's what blogs are for

When I was growing up in Boston in the 1980's, you'd just see way more people out on the streets on summer evenings because it was too freaking hot to do anything inside and people were damn bored. That's when the trouble would start.
People are less politically engaged. Partly it's because Congress sucks, but partly – yes – it's because people are playing Candy Crush!
It is a pacifier for grown-ups, a nationwide babysitting service that keeps people in line.

We have trained an entire population to take refuge in a pointless activity that doesn't serve any real purpose except to distract us and to mollify us, not to mention collect our data for later marketing purposes.
If all the apps and all the video games stopped working for a few weeks, what would happen? What would people do with themselves?
key_insight: Smartphones and gaming have become a mass pacification tool that reduces both crime and civic engagement by giving people an endless escape from boredom and each other.
